candidate_problems.text: Does the 'informational equilibrium lock-in' picture add explanatory content beyond standard decoherence/einselection, or is it only a redescription? The key unresolved tension is whether one can define the proposed equilibrium, stability, and 'costly to reverse' conditions mathematically enough to derive definite quasi-classical records and distinguish this conjecture from existing Everettian, decoherence, quantum Darwinist, or consistent-histories accounts.

How, if at all, can this framework explain single-outcome actuality rather than merely branch-relative stability? The conjecture shifts from collapse to observer-relative lock-in, but an open question remains whether that really solves the measurement problem or only explains why observers inside each branch experience definiteness while leaving unresolved why one outcome is experienced rather than all decohered outcomes existing. This is the central tension between emergent definiteness and ontology.

Can the Born rule be derived from a measure over accessible equilibria without circularly presupposing amplitudes, observers, or coarse-graining choices? The conjecture proposes that probabilities reflect the availability of equilibria from the pre-lock-in state, but it is unclear what fixes that measure, why it should equal squared amplitude, and how the result avoids dependence on arbitrary partitioning of branches or records.
